data.title: HOOW: Risk-Off Could Lead To Losses In This High-Risk Trading Platform
data.description:
The Roundhill HOOD WeeklyPay ETF (HOOW) is an actively managed, leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with weekly exposure to the performance of Robinhood Markets (HOOD) through the use of weekly total return swaps. With 120% exposure to HOOD, HOOW can be used by long-term investors seeking amplified exposure and income. Despite the appeal of the fund, I believe there may be some risks relating to the underlying shares that may be reflected in HOOW, particularly when considering the weakening consumer market environment, leading me to recommend this strategy with a Sell rating.

Investment Thesis

Stating the obvious, Robinhood is largely a retail investment platform providing investment brokerage services that cater to the market through various products like crypto, options, and now the prediction markets. While broadening its services designed for retail traders and investors, this platform may face macroeconomic risks, particularly if the market correction were to intensify.

Robinhood’s services tend to promote riskier assets like options and crypto trading, a high-risk, high-reward market that can either greatly benefit a customer or result in substantial losses, depending on their abilities as a trader. This matters because these products can be challenging to trade in an uncertain, highly volatile market environment and may result in a weakening customer base if the market were to remain bearish.

At a high level, consumer sentiment weakened in March 2026 following the US invasion of Iran, leading to heightened concerns relating to inflation and the broader economic outlook. While a proportionate amount of the concerns stem from higher oil prices, which have climbed above $100/bbl, the heightened prices may ripple throughout the economy, impacting industries like transport and retail, amongst others. A more direct impact would be the price of gasoline, which has trended higher in recent weeks, following the path of oil.

While funded customers increased in Q4’25, net deposits presented unevenness throughout FY25 with a year-over-year decline in Q4’25 relative to Q4’24. While one data point shouldn’t be considered as a trendsetting component, I suspect that Q4’25 results coincide with the broader impact of inflation, particularly at the height of seasonal holiday spending as well as the end of the calendar year. This translated to a sequential decline of customer assets in Q4’25 across all asset classes.

One factor that may have contributed to this matter was the sharp decline in the price of Bitcoin in October 2025, resulting in the liquidation of $19b of leveraged crypto accounts in a single day. With Robinhood’s customer base being heavily centric on riskier assets, I suspect the sharp market decline in crypto may have led to a shift in investor tone towards the asset class that may trickle into other assets if the market continues to decline.

While this may be an unfair assessment of Robinhood, I have reason to believe that the trading platform has a higher concentration of risky investors relative to traditional brokerages like Schwab (SCHW) or Fidelity, given Robinhood’s KPIs tied to options volumes, crypto, and futures.

Given these factors, HOOW may be inadvertently negatively impacted by the decline of HOOD shares driven by the challenging market conditions. While Roundhill’s WeeklyPay strategies provide a certain appeal as a potential long-term income investment strategy for amplified performance, the underlying security may continue to pressure the performance of HOOW.

About Roundhill HOOD WeeklyPay ETF

HOOW was launched by Roundhill on June 18, 2025, on the Cboe BZX Exchange. The strategy has an expense ratio of 99bps, aligned with peer leveraged and derivative-driven income strategies.

HOOW has $107mm in net assets, with an average of $8.24mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

HOOW was designed to provide investors with 1.2x the weekly performance of HOOD shares through the use of weekly swap derivatives. Rather than a daily reset in typical leveraged strategies, HOOW’s performance is dependent on the closing price of HOOD shares on the last trading day of the calendar week. While investors will still be exposed to similar structural risks like compounded returns and NAV erosion, I believe the elongated reset period can reduce these risks and allow for an extended holding period by investors.

Despite the weekly performance reset, HOOW shares generally reflect the targeted 1.2x daily performance of HOOD.

The value of HOOW is the weekly income component of the fund, providing investors with regular distributions based on the performance of the underlying assets. Investors should be aware that 100% of the distribution is derived from return of capital, creating a tradeoff between capital appreciation when investing in HOOD and income when investing in HOOW, net of the 1.2x leverage risk. Given the decline in HOOD share price and the relative decline in the distribution, I do not believe historical distributions will provide a significant gauge for understanding the fund, let alone what to expect for future performance.

Risks Related to HOOW

HOOW is a 1.2x leveraged income-oriented strategy designed to provide investors with weekly distributions based on the performance of the weekly swap derivatives. Investors will be exposed to compounded performance when investing in HOOW, potentially resulting in unsalvageable losses if negative performance were to persist over extended periods. Investors may also be exposed to significant NAV erosion due to the structure of the fund.

For example, if HOOD were to return 5% by the end of the calendar week on a $100 investment in HOOW, the investor would close the week at $106. If HOOD were to decline by -5% the following week, the investment would decline to $99.94, not reduce to $100.

If investors actively trade HOOW, the investor may be exposed to greater taxes on short-term capital gains on top of expenses and trading fees.

Given the risks involved in investing in leveraged strategies, investors should adhere to strict risk management protocols and sell discipline in order to protect their initial investment. Compounded losses may not be recoverable by extending out a holding period.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

HOOW presents investors with 1.2x the performance of HOOD shares while returning weekly income in the form of return of capital. While Roundhill’s weekly leveraged strategies provide a certain appeal to income-oriented investors, I have reason to believe that HOOW may face further decline as a result of the macroeconomic challenges faced by HOOD shares. I am recommending HOOW with a Sell rating.

data.title: GigaCloud: Wall Street Still Sleeps On This Hidden E-Commerce Gem
data.description:
Thesis Update on GCT

Just like in January, and throughout multiple bullish updates on the stock from my end, I believe GigaCloud Technology (GCT) is one of the clearest risk/reward plays on the market these days, although it's exposed to geopolitical risks to some extent. With the ongoing diversification in the European market and the rise of higher-margin segments within the sales structure, GCT is very likely to grow at over-consensus rates in the next couple of years (at least). I see an upside potential of ~52% ahead, so I keep my "Buy" rating unchanged.

Figuring Out GCT's Prospects

As I'm sure people familiar with GCT stock know, the company operates in the cross-border B2B e-commerce market for large, bulky goods, and for the past few years, they've managed to secure the leading place in this niche. GCT is running a proprietary SFR model (supplier-fulfilled retailing) that helps handle the complex logistics of goods like furniture, fitness equipment, and large appliances, expanding quite rapidly in the US and Europe despite all the macro headwinds.

In Q4 2025, GCT had $362.7 million in sales (+22.7% YoY) and also delivered an adjusted EPS of $1.04 (+37% YoY), thus beating on both the top and bottom lines by 9.15% and over 80%, respectively. The market initially thought that GCT would lose almost 1/3 in terms of EPS, but the firm clearly proved that its margins remain more reliable than the case with other industry companies that focus more on smaller parcel logistics.

What contributed to the margins' stability was GCT's successful transition from 1P retailers to a higher-margin cohort of 3P platform, where GMV is growing quite rapidly as well. In fiscal 2025, for example, 3P seller GMV was $851.2 million (+22% YoY), and the number of active 3P sellers went up by 16.9% to 1,299. Against the backdrop of the average spend per active buyer being stable at over $130,000, we see an extreme stickiness that drives up the top line at reasonably high margins, which is great to see.

The pivot to European markets seems to be working as well. The revenue chunk in that region went up by 68% YoY for the full year, and it was a massive growth driver for the consolidated results. The YoY expansion stayed consistent throughout the year - in Q4 alone, we've seen EU product revenue rise by 64% to $98 million, so the growth rates aren't decelerating much. I believe we can expect the same trend to continue well into 2026-2027. Why? In addition to the organic expansion planned, GCT signed a marketplace initiative with the Otto Group recently, which is Europe's largest e-commerce and retail entity.

So, instead of acquiring customers one by one, as we could have thought initially, GCT is going to become an infrastructure provider in the EU, connecting Asian supply with the EU demand. Again, the commissions and fees from this initiative are going to drive further margin expansion, in my opinion.

Analysts had no other choice but to raise their EPS estimates for the next few quarters after GCT's stunning Q4 results:

The stock's price action reacted positively to all that, having risen by over 30% on the earnings release day and tightly consolidating close to the 2024 highs ever since:

I believe the price action's strength is very likely to continue further as the management keeps executing on the medium-term plans.

Given that 54% of the GMV now comes from 3P sellers, where margins are structurally higher, the firm's capital intensity should become lower. In other words, the high-margin commissions and logistics fees they collect should bring in massive FCFs without the great inventory risk that 1P sales have. I expect to see a widening network effect as the active buyer/seller base scales, and if so, the whole unit economics structure will improve - more margin expansion ahead.

I also like the inorganic part of GCT's story. On January 1, 2026, they closed the acquisition of New Classic Home Furnishings for $18 million in cash, which looks like a small deal, but it's a step into brick-and-mortar furniture retail with already 1,000+ active retailers. The integration is going to take 4-6 quarters, per management, but once it is done in 2027, I believe GCT will effectively expand beyond just e-commerce and reach into physical storefronts, thus creating a true omnichannel ecosystem.

The execution is always a risk, but we can look at the past to figure out the odds of a successful integration from GCT's management team. When GigaCloud bought Noble House in late 2023, the target company was close to bankruptcy, losing ~$40 million a year. GCT said it would leverage the firm's superior marketplace model and optimize the SKU portfolio, and so they did. After retiring 3,800 outdated SKUs and bringing in 1,200 new in-demand products, the Noble House portfolio came back to profitability, even ahead of the initial target date. By Q4, NH's portfolio was delivering over 40% YoY revenue growth, which looks spectacular to me, and I believe it says a lot about the odds of a well-managed turnaround at New Classic Home Furnishings as well.

Another thing that I like about GCT is the shareholders' returns, which remain strong thanks to the generous $111 million, three-year share repurchase program the Board authorized in August last year. Throughout 2025, GCT bought back $33 million worth of stock, and I think they'll continue to do so as long as the stock looks cheap. For me, it still looks cheap - let me show you why.

GigaCloud's Valuation Update

Right now, GCT is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of ~11.4x (forward basis), with the consensus seeing only a 9.54% YoY expansion in FY2026:

The management hasn't issued a formal guidance for FY2026, but they expect to have $330-355 million in sales for the upcoming Q1 2026. It's $342.5 million at the midpoint, so the YoY growth to what we saw in FY2025 is going to be at ~25.96%. That's almost exactly what analysts price in for Q1, but as we can see, they still anticipate a massive deceleration starting in Q2 and continuing to at least late FY2027.

I don't think GCT's top-line growth will decelerate so fast - the 3P sellers transition, the firm's platform revenue expansion, and the EU opportunity look promising. What I don't understand as well is the priced-in deceleration in EPS growth. With the points that I discussed above in terms of margin expansion, even if they don't fully materialize, the bottom line should remain stable, and the existing operating leverage is likely to hold.

I assume that GCT will be able to reach the higher end of its Q1 guidance, and the current FY2027 EPS projection will be off by 20%, given the continuation in GCT's EBIT margin expansion. So, instead of $4.20 FY2027 EPS currently priced in, I believe we should see something closer to $5.04. The odds of seeing these beats look high to me, and the market likes stories like this, so the stock is likely to remain trading at 12-15x earnings in 1-2 years from now. If so, it implies a price target of $68.04/share:

$5.04 * ((12x+15x)/2)) = $68.04 /share

Source: Oakoff's calculations

GCT is currently at $44.78/share, so my PT implies an upside potential of almost 52% in the next 1-2 years.

Risks

The trade war is perhaps one of the biggest risks to my GCT's bullish thesis, as this is what we can't truly control and quantify. Section 301 tariffs on Chinese goods remain in effect, and the Trump Administration keeps imposing new tariffs.

Since GCT's supply chain is tied to sourcing from Asia, any unexpected statutory tariffs announced shortly would hit shipping volumes and gross margins, thus leading to lower EPS growth for years ahead. In Q4, we've already seen a 3% decline in service margins, which was due to peak-season ground fulfillment surcharges and lower ocean spot rates. But it can drop much lower with new tariffs and potentially lower demand from the retailers.

Your Takeaway

Despite some clear risks, I like management's execution so far over the past few years and the fact that they've managed to somehow deliver stable margins in today's harsh environment for the logistics sector as a whole. The M&As they do usually add value, and I believe that will be the case with their new purchase (New Classic Home Furnishings) as well. The EU expansion should help them offset some of the potential US weakness. From a valuation standpoint, the stock still looks quite cheap - I see an upside of over 50%.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Lloyds Banking: I Like It, But I'd Want It Cheaper
data.description:
I've been reviewing more and more UK-based businesses as of late. I intend to continue with this here and provide coverage and a price target for Lloyds (LYG), one of the more well-known banks in the UK. The company is one of what in the UK are known as the "big four", the banks with histories going back around 250 years. While Lloyds may no longer be the global bank that it once was, it remains, to date, the UK's largest retail bank with over 25M customers.

Its legacy and reputation today are buffered by the fact that it has a very extensive network of high-street branches, aside from operating brands like both Halifax and Bank of Scotland.

Lloyds was once part of financing the industrial revolution; today, it's the nation's leading digital bank and mortgage bank. When it comes to banks and what banks I invest in, I focus on things related to conservative fundamentals. I want a good return on assets, good returns, conservative lending policies, and low credit losses (and preferably also provisions for credit losses, being able to sort of see what the company does in terms of being conservative).

With Lloyds Banking Group, native ticker LLOY, we have a lot of that from the get-go. An A rating, a market capitalisation of almost £60B, and a relatively competitive yield of about 3.7%. The company mostly hits its marks and forecasts in terms of earnings growth.

The main challenge with Lloyds is trusting in growth. I say this because for more than 10 years, the company has been in what can be generously called "a slump". This slump has basically seen flat earnings. The recent company AEPS of £0.07 was something the bank had 10 years ago and more. It went negative for several years during the GFC.

A big part of this article will be explaining why Lloyds, given these trends, can even be considered attractive.

Lloyds - Where It Came From in 2007 and How It Turned Around

It's somewhat fair to say that Lloyds massive decline during the financial crisis was not entirely of its own making. The banking group was the subject of a government-encouraged takeover of one of its peers, HBOS, by the Prime Minister at the time, Gordon Brown, who was seen as personally encouraging the deal to avoid the need to nationalize one of the largest banks in the UK, HBOS. Of course, blame can and should be laid at the feet of LLOY management as well. It was a two-way street, but the blame should be more resoundingly levied at the party that suspended competition regulation and statutes, which was a requirement for this deal to even go through. We can also add the regulator (FSA) and the Central Bank, the Bank of England, being supportive of the deal.

This is what turned Lloyds Bank into the Lloyds Banking Group that we have today - and it's also what led to the 10+ years of underperformance that we see today.

Because as a result of the deal, Lloyds comparatively well-managed books and risks were laden with the toxic debt and assets from HBOS, which not only turned out to be bad but also had over £10B of losses revealed in early 2009. The merger even failed to materialize safely because the government needed to step in, not nationalizing by majority, but taking a 43%+ stake in the new banking group to save it. That, and the 2008 PPI scandal.

The company was paying that one off 12 years later. So to say that these events rocked the faith in British banking is a conservative perspective.

And there you have the unfortunately poorly managed legacy of how this current ticker came to be. It echoes of the crisis on the other side of the pond, and I believe this is the main reason why LLOY has been unable to get out of its slump for many, many years.

Until now?

That is still the question. There are clear signs, dear readers, that things may finally be changing at Lloyds. Understand that fundamental safety, with A- credit, is now solid.

Other signs that piqued my interest were a significant rise in EBIT - 12% up to over £6.6B for the 2025A period, with upgraded 2026 guidance (which was rare), and a £1.75B buyback (which I don't consider that great, but more on that below).

It seems to me that multiple factors are driving KPIs, which could finally see this bank more clearly rise from the ashes. Net income growth and NIM were solid, and the company's costs, thanks to digital transformation and efficiencies, are starting to look similar to peers. The once heavily mortgage-laden revenue stream has become more diversified with wealth management and insurance, and it now represents more than 30% of the company's income. Compared to previously, that is healthy diversification, and it acts as an obvious buffer against the threat of falling interest rates, which would otherwise work to balance it "downward" again. The company's C/I still isn't "good" - it's getting there - its goal is below 50%, which is a requirement today, but the mere fact that the company is making progress is interesting enough to me here.

But to understand why things might grow, we must understand why things may not. We must understand what has previously kept the bank down, unlike other European or international banks, or for that matter, say Erste Bank, which I also cover and which is exposed to Eastern Europe.

Why is it that Lloyds is so "low" here and has been for so long?

Lloyds is, unfortunately, the bank with a primary correlation to the UK economy, and that economy hasn't been "flying" for the past decade. This makes the AEPS growth directly tied to downturns in the UK economy, and it extremely limits all growth opportunities. Because the UK is a very saturated, mature banking market. Following BREXIT, it has also seen competition from other banking havens, such as Frankfurt, Berlin, and Paris.

For much of the past few years, low interest rates in the UK have compressed NIM for the bank, while market entrants with asset-light operations and good interest rates on savings have pressured the company's deposit markets and rates. Revenue growth has been close to nonexistent or very challenging - in part because Lloyds has had its own problems, and in part because of the market.

And its expansion into non-mortgage business has not been without its challenges. The expansion into motor finance and insurance has not been without its risks. Obviously, as I pointed out in my article on Ally Financial, motor financing companies with higher NCOs, higher NPLs, and other potential negatives. Then Lloyds was accused of mis-selling provisions, though it was far from the only player to be accused of this.

You can see, looking at Lloyds Banking Group, that the group consists of a number of different trusted, UK-centric brands. But in the end, I believe it's fair to characterize the focus and the correlations as I do here. When things go well or fair in the UK, the company will do well. When they don't, the company has a harder time. Trying to de-correlate from interest rates and CPI is hard, but the company is doing it as well as it can.

I think it's also entirely fair to say that the 2025 results are showing strong growth, to the point where the anaemic return pattern of the last 15-or-so-years may be broken. But at the same time, some analysts are forecasting massive, double-digit growth rates for the next few years, ending with a more than doubling of the AEPS in 2028E.

This needs to be qualified.

As I understand the forecasts, having read the reports and assumptions, the path to £0.14/share is given by growth initiatives (WM and motor) such as digital and AI, NIM and NII growth (why, given the tendency to lower rates, I am unsure), and its pivot into corporate banking. To my mind, the logic underlying the assumptions given is weak. They have a characteristic exuberance because they don't account for, in my view, costs for these measures, and they don't properly (again, in my view) account for the higher risks from their expansion targets, such as motor. It's already clear from regulatory action in motor that profit from this is unlikely to be as high as the estimates currently show.

To that comes the issue of buybacks. I believe management did the company a disservice with the £1.5B program. As usual, I believe the company is favoring short-term mechanical KPI improvement over actual, under-the-hood improvements to business operations. This was especially true before 2025, when the company was facing a significant plunge in profit. The bank sets aside billions for covering the shortfalls and risk in motor scandals, but at the same time uses hard-earned cash for artificial pushes. The best that the bank can hope for with this, which, by the way, is also what I argue has already happened, is for shares to become short-term overvalued as a result of this strategy.

I would have preferred this capital to go to risk management and provisions, or perhaps investing further in its own operations - which there seems to be no shortage of.

With that, I look at the company's valuation.

Lloyds Banking Group - Upside From Valuation, but Not Necessarily This One

So, the company has generally traded around the £0.6-£0.7/share level for some time. Recently, it climbed to above £1/share, only to then promptly decline again. As I am writing the article, there seems to be a movement up for the stock again.

How do I interpret this?

Two ways: first, I see impacts of share buybacks starting from 2024 and through 2026. Secondly, I believe the bank's rise is actually congruent with the underlying earnings growth, the real improvements sans buybacks that the bank has made.

So, the picture is mixed. I like what I am seeing because I do see one of the legacy banks of England slowly rise back to where I believe they "should" be trading, ideally. At the same time, the bank seems to be a little overeager to get back up there, with buybacks ahead of much of its actual improvement or growth, and not really done at a conservatively attractive valuation.

It is indisputable to me that LLOY is better-positioned and managed than it has been for many years. It is further clear to me that the bank represents a compelling buy at the right entry. The problem is that the targets the bank has set and the expectations for how this will work out are too exuberant for my taste. There is insufficient data to state or estimate with clarity that this is indeed how things will go.

Even if I were to use the 10-year average of 8-9x P/E, which is fair for a bank with the risk profile of Lloyds (9-12x P/E once it truly turns around), that return does not breach double digits annualized in RoR. It's about 4% per year, without the dividend. Even at almost 11x P/E, the return is not 14% without the dividend - and I believe forecasting the company at 11x with the AEPS growth that's being expected (implying a £1.4/share price) is far too optimistic.

I will stay conservative and therefore discount/moderate the company's growth by about 50% (as I believe it to be unrealistic). I will further use the 8-9.5x P/E average, with a 2028E of 10x to allow for some upside. This comes to a PT of about £0.8/share for a 15% annualized upside.

This price target directly reflects the concern for UK housing and the correlation it has to these markets and slowdowns, as well as the potential for stagnant or slowing interest rates. I also forecast a fair bit of NIM compression and take into account the motor headwind.

From that perspective, I view it as fair and give you the following thesis.

The ADR target for the company's LYG ticker is therefore $4.35/share.

Thesis

Lloyds Banking Group is one of the legacy and premier banking houses of the UK. It has many attractive and well-managed brands, and the improvements seen since 2009 are clear - but not yet enough to make me invest at this price. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a £0.8/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of £0.8/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Household Survey Shows A YTD Loss Of 1.4M Jobs
data.description:
Originally published on April 4, 2026

The analysis below covers the employment picture released on the first Friday of every month. While most of the attention goes to the Headline Report, it can be helpful to look at the details, revisions, and other reports to get a better gauge of what is really going on.

Current Trends

The jobs report showed a gain of 178k jobs for the month of March. While that beat expectations, it will most likely be revised down in future months. Furthermore, the Household Survey showed a loss of 64k jobs. This is another consistent trend: the Household Survey continuously underperforming the Headline Report.

Figure 1: Primary Report vs. Household Survey – Monthly

To show the magnitude of this underperformance, consider the chart below. YTD, the Headline Report shows a gain of 205k jobs, whereas the Household Survey shows a loss of 1.4M jobs.

Figure 2: Primary Report vs. Household Survey – Annual

The BLS publishes the data behind their Birth/Death assumptions (formation of new business). The data showed that the BLS assumed a job loss of 47k jobs in March.

Figure 3: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth/Death Assumptions – Monthly

For the year, the birth/death assumption is -18k jobs.

Figure 4: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth Death Assumptions – Monthly

The big report released earlier this month is the Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW). According to the BLS, this is a far more accurate and rigorous report covering 95% of jobs available at a highly detailed level. Due to the rigor, the report is released quarterly on a several month lag.

The latest report is for Q3 2025. As shown, July and September were both below the Headline Report.

Figure 5: Primary Report vs QCEW – Yearly

This QCEW data reveals that the economy lost jobs through 3 quarters in 2025. The revised Headline Report also shows this job loss, but the QCEW shows an even more negative picture of -633k (vs. -560k for the Headline Report). This just pours salt in the wound of an already terrible story.

Figure 6: Primary Report vs QCEW – Yearly

Digging Into the Headline Report

Unfortunately, despite being highly unreliable, the Headline report is the best data we have for the more recent periods. Furthermore, this is the data the Fed uses to shape its policy. The 178k jobs gained is the third best month since September 2024.

Figure 7: Change by sector

Jobs by Category

When looking at the last 12-month trend, you can see that every job category was above trend except for Other. As for the trend, 5 of 8 categories are actually negative over the last year.

Figure 8: Current vs. TTM

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the numbers.

Figure 9: Labor Market Detail

Revisions

This is the biggest story of the jobs report. After all the revisions, the job picture is significantly bleaker than the data originally showed. Every month shown below saw a negative revision except for January of this year. For February, the original 92k jobs lost has been revised to a loss of 133k.

Figure 10: Revisions

Over the last twelve months, jobs have been revised down by about 90k per month!

Figure 11: Revisions

More Detail in the Household Survey

Another level of detail in the household report shows full-time vs. part-time job holders. The data shows full-time jobs being added in March.

Figure 12: Full-Time vs. Part-Time

Historical Perspective

The chart below shows data going back to 1955.

Figure 13: Historical Labor Market

The labor force participation rate is still well below the highs before the Global Financial Crisis. This month showed it falling to 61.9%, the lowest level since 2021.

Figure 14: Labor Market Distribution

Conclusion

Ignore the unexpected positive job growth this month. The full data shows a very negative picture.

  • Household Survey: recession level ugly
  • QCEW shows 633k jobs lost in the first 3 quarters of 2025
  • Revisions: constantly moving downward
  • Labor force participation falls to a multi-year low

This is the sign of an economy hanging by a thread.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Radian Group: Diversification Benefits Are Underappreciated
data.description:
Shares of Radian Group (RDN) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 11%. That said, shares are off their highs amid concerns about the strength of the U.S. housing market, given its exposure to home prices as a private mortgage insurer (“PMI”). However, with a recently closed acquisition, RDN’s business is much more diverse today than it was 12 months ago. I last covered the company in November, rating the stock a “Buy,” and shares are essentially flat since then. With Radian having completed its Inigo acquisition and a revised macro backdrop, now is a good time to revisit shares.

Critically, Radian spent $1.67 billion for Inigo, completing the acquisition in early February. With this purchase, Radian is a much more diversified business, moving beyond its existing PMI business to owning a member within the Lloyd’s of London syndicate. With new exposure to specialty property casualty insurance lines, Radian’s results will be less impacted by movements in the housing market. It will continue to run Inigo as a standalone unit out of London given the difference in the businesses. I see few, if any, cost synergies, with the primary value being the increased diversity of the company, which should improve the resilience of its cash flow.

As a reminder, Inigo operates a diversified business across various segments in both insurance and reinsurance. Just over a third of its business is tied to reinsurance, and there is meaningful catastrophe risk. As such, Radian now has more exposure to hurricanes and other natural disasters, things that were previously little more than afterthoughts. We have seen pricing pressure in the reinsurance segment as more capital has gone into the space.

As such, I expect Inigo’s go-forward combined ratio to be closer to 90%-92% from historic levels in the upper 80% range. This implies run-rate earnings of $150 million, which is a bit less than the ~$170 million management has spoken about based on historic results. RDN funded this purchase by selling down a portion of its investment portfolio, likely reducing net investment income and increasing interest expense by a combined ~$80-$90 million. As such, I expect about $60-$70 million of run-rate earnings benefit from this acquisition, or about $0.35 of EPS.

While I expect Inigo to run at tighter margins than in the past, it still is a positive contributor to EPS, given RDN is deploying its excess capital and working down excess liquidity. The timing is also opportune in my view, with its legacy business likely having passed its peak in profitability. In the company’s fourth quarter, reported on February 18, Radian Group earned $1.16 per share, which was $0.07 better than expected. In 2025, income from continuing operations fell by $42 million to $618 million; EPS was down just over 1% to $4.39 as the decline in the share count offset much of the decline in net income. The increase in loss provisioning essentially offset the benefit from a reduced share count.

I have written previously that this industry was passing the peak in its profitability. Thanks to the rise in home prices from 2020 to 2023, historic policies had very few losses, enabling years of material reserve releases. After years of releases, there simply are fewer reserves to release, which mechanistically lowers profits. To be clear, current underwriting quality results are still solid, and profits remain excellent. We are just seeing a retracement from an unsustainably high level of earnings.

As of Q4, 2.56% of loans have defaulted, up only 12bps from last year. 90% of defaults from a year ago have been cured, and given the large equity cushion in most homes, I expect this elevated cure rate to continue at least through 2026, leaving actual credit costs at a very small fraction of its default rate. In Q4, it provisioned $22 million for losses after having no net reserves a year ago, and total reserves are now $400 million.

This year-over-year increase in provisions speaks to the declining tailwind of releases from prior-year reserves, which have already been brought down substantially. In Q4, its mortgage insurance in force was up 3% from a year earlier to $283 billion, and the growth in new policies is contributing to the rise in provisions (from extremely low levels).

Home prices are up about 11% since the end of 2022. Because RDN only pays a claim when a policyholder defaults and the home is worth less than the remaining mortgage, the combination of low unemployment and higher prices has greatly reduced losses. With higher prices, a homeowner from 2022 or earlier who cannot make the payment can likely sell their home for more than the mortgage. Because of this rise, I view all policies written in 2021 and earlier as having essentially no risk of loss, 2022 policies as having just modest risk, and 2023-present policies as having normal risk of loss.

A year ago, only ~34% of policies were written after 2022. As of 12/31/25, 47% of policies were written after 2022. With each passing day, the back book becomes a smaller part of the business as old policies pay down and RDN writes new policies. As a result of this, its policies are becoming a bit riskier over time, as its least-risky policies mature. This means the company is more exposed to a downturn in the housing market than it was 12 months ago.

Now, I remain relatively cautious on housing, given extremely challenged affordability. With the tick-up in long-term rates, mortgage rates appear set to stay higher for longer, and I expect the spring selling season to be a relative bust. I expect home prices to fall 0%-3% this year, with the Sun Belt underperforming. Given the ongoing decline in construction, I view a sharper decline as unlikely, barring a moderate recession. I do not view a decline like this as likely to lead to material losses, but I expect mortgage insurance net income to decline again in 2026 as provisions are higher YoY.

Turning to its balance sheet, Radian ended the year with a $6 billion investment portfolio. Amid all the concerns around private credit, it is important to note that RDN has a very conservative investment portfolio. 27% of the portfolio matures inside a year, and a quarter of its portfolio is in government or agency debt. While it does own CLOs, most are AAA rated, and that tranche has never taken a loss. Over 90% of its portfolio is A-rated or higher. I do not see a risk of RDN suffering material credit losses.

The company ended the year with $1.8 billion of holding company liquidity, well above the $500-$750 million I like to see, as it prepared to close the Inigo acquisition. This was substantially reduced subsequent to year-end with the Inigo purchase. Debt-to-capital leverage ended the year at 18%, and it should get back below 20% by year-end after a temporary rise. Importantly, Radian is in the process of selling its title and services business as it sheds noncore assets and can use proceeds to further strengthen its financial position after completing the Inigo acquisition. I expect noncore assets to fetch at least $500 million, given ~$55 million of earnings power.

These proceeds will help RDN restart buybacks after a brief pause. During 2026, the company expects to receive $600 million in dividends from its holding company, Radian Guaranty. As a reminder, it is the holding company that does buybacks and pays the dividend, and it is dependent upon dividends from the operating company to make these capital returns. These payments can fully cover its dividend, and shares offer a 3% dividend yield. I also expect RDN to use proceeds to pay down $200 million of debt taken out to fund the Inigo acquisition.

We could see a resumption of share buybacks in the second half of the year. During 2025, it bought back about 8% of its shares, spending $430 million. I expect buybacks of no more than $200 million this year and then a return to the 2025 pace in 2027. Importantly, ongoing reserve releases from the roll-off of existing policies provide $450-500 million in annual reserve releases, supporting intercompany dividends. Plus, the insurance operating company ended the year with $1.6 billion of excess assets, leaving it with a solid financial position.

Book value ended the year at $35.29, or nearly $37 excluding the $282 million in unrealized losses on its portfolio that sit in AOCI. Given the rise in rates and lessened expectation from rate cuts, I expect this loss to narrow more slowly than previously, though as bonds pull to par and mature, its AOCI loss should decline over time. Reinvesting maturing bonds that have lower yields should be a tailwind for net investment income.

I believe the company should trade at least at book value ex-AOCI. With ~$4-$4.25 of earnings from its core business and a ~$0.35 benefit from Inigo, I expect RDN to earn about $4.40-$4.50 this year, leaving shares at less than 8x earnings. This is a very attractive valuation in my view. Much of RDN’s PMI exposure is still loss-remote, and it is less exposed to housing than before. As we see Inigo consolidated into results, I expect shares to migrate towards $40, and a sale of noncore assets can hasten the speed of buybacks, creating a further catalyst for shares. With over 15% upside, I maintain a "Buy" on Radian.

data.title: AGNCN: The Sensible Middle Ground In AGNC's Preferred Stack
data.description:
Introduction

AGNC Investment Corp. CUM 1/1000 7% C (AGNCN) is among a group of AGNC Investment Corp.’s preferred shares with current yields in the high single-digit range. The preferreds are backed by AGNC’s Agency MBS strategy, a strong business that delivered 22.7% economic return in 2025 and 34.8% total return. The mREIT environment is stabilizing, and along with that, the high current income has made AGNC’s preferred stack popular among investors.

The problem is how to choose from among the list of AGNC preferreds. There are various types of coupons here, all trading in a tight band around their $25 par value. On the surface, the decision appears simple enough - get the one with the highest yield. But that ignores two things: how tightly each issue tends to trade around par and call risk. These things have an impact on forward returns.

I prefer AGNCN because it is the most balanced one here. My income philosophy rebels against yield-chasing. I like my income portfolio to fulfill my needs, not my wants. AGNC does not ask you to pay too much for yield but still delivers strong income—one that falls right within my preferred income bracket.

What AGNC Really is

AGNC’s leverage is a hefty ~7.2x tangible equity at quarter-end. Its portfolio consists of agency mortgage-backed securities. Leverage has reduced a little from earlier quarters as the MBS industry has stabilized. AGNC’s business model is simply this: it borrows at short-term repo rates, invests in long-duration agency MBS, hedges against interest rate hikes using derivatives like interest rate swaps and swaptions, and pockets the spread between the borrowing rate and the MBS yield.

While this all looks easy enough, the income is very sensitive to interest rate changes and mortgage spreads. It works well when these are stable—when they are not, book value gets compressed, and so do returns.

This model worked well last year. There was a shift towards lower rates, and mortgage spreads also tightened—so AGNC performed well. Even better, AGNC notes that things are also looking up in the current period.

So then, what you are effectively getting when you buy AGNC—whether common or preferred—is exposure to an MBS carry situation that is stabilizing today. Hence, the difference between common and preferred gets to be more crucial now. The commons are directly exposed to book value fluctuations. The preferreds are more protected.

AGNC’s recent performance has shown us it is investable. The question is how best to invest in AGNC—which brings us to the preferred stack and their individual differences.

The AGNC Preferred Stack: Same Issuer, Different Trade-Offs

AGNC’s current preferred stack—from series C to H—looks similar on the surface—same issuer, same income strategy, similarly located in the capital structure. They also all generate yields within a tight range.

Yet these differ on three important things—coupon/yield level, price vs. $25 par, and structure—whether fixed or floating, when and how they reset.

The Full Stack with original coupon rates

What This Table Shows

The decision looks trivial on the surface—just buy the highest yielder. That changes, however, once you think about the pricing and structure. Higher coupon names like AGNCL and AGNCZ trade closer to par, compressing upside and bringing in call risk. Lower coupon issues like AGNCP (and AGNCM to an extent) tend to trade at discounts. But then a discount exists because yield is lower.

At current pricing, AGNCO is near par and sits in the middle—neither discounted enough nor yielding enough to deserve being paid close to $25.

What we have here is essentially not a yield comparison—that would be too easy. This is actually a capital allocation problem within a set of very similar instruments, each with individual constraints.

Since these securities share the same issuer and the same risk, investment preference must be based on three questions:

  1. What’s the income?

  2. What am I paying for it?

  3. What are my risks?

The market mostly focuses on the first question—simply because that is the more outwardly attractive feature. But as we will see here, the real difference comes from questions two and three. AGNCN stands out here, not because it shows a distinct advantage at specific times, but because over a long duration, it tends to sit between the extremes. It is more balanced and avoids the weaknesses of the others.

Broadly, the market tends not to price them consistently. Some of the price differences can be explained by their structural differences, small changes in yield, price to par, and risk profile. However, the market often treats these securities as substitutes and tends to apply a rotation behavior instead of using a proper framework.

Why I Reject the Alternatives and Pick AGNCN

That proper framework is yield, price relative to par, and risk. Once we have that right, we can start eliminating from the list. We will then see that each of the other series except AGNCN leans too far in one direction or the other.

Here’s the normalized framework: Par = $25 Annual dividend = coupon × $25 Current yield = this varies with the market and the price and floating/reset terms.

Now, let’s do the elimination step by step.

First, take the high coupon plays - AGNCL and AGNCZ. These tend to trade close to par—sometimes above it—over time. So your return profile becomes: Return = yield, with limited upside + call risk

With these, you are effectively locking yourself into both high income and limiting your ability to make a profit if prices improve, because the issuer may call at that time. On top of that, you are also paying a higher entry price.

Now look at the low coupon item - AGNCP. This is discounted, but for a reason - lower income. Across different market conditions, the income gap between this and mid-coupon issues like AGNCN tends to persist. On the other hand, the discount doesn’t always persist in a predictable manner.

So the trade becomes low income, uncertain capital appreciation due to uncertain discount dynamics. The trade only works if the price moves up towards par value—this gives you capital gain. Or if the macro environment is such that rates fall and spreads tighten, and you believe price appreciation will give you a better return than income. That is not true in a carry-driven environment.

One other issue is AGNCO. This sits in the middle, trading near par across cycles, and doesn’t offer either a good discount or a superior income.

What remains are AGNCM and AGNCN. Across different parts of the cycle, AGNCM offers a greater discount but lower income. AGNCN offers higher income but a more moderate discount. So you either buy AGNCM and get chances of more upside, or you buy AGNCN with its stronger carry and balanced pricing. Since income (carry) is more dominant in the current macro conditions, I prefer AGNCN.

One caveat to note in this analysis: Look at the total return for all six instruments:

As you can see, they don’t always converge. Especially the middle ones, like AGNCM and AGNCO, tend to move across zones. Also, the differences in when these were launched also make analysis difficult.

Conclusion

Because they look so similar, investors may find it difficult to choose from among the AGNC preferreds, even though AGNC is well-regarded as a stable MBS business. However, if you look at the details—coupon, price relative to par, and structural positioning—you can see the differences.

Some series are heavy on the yield, but that comes at a premium price. Others offer discounts but generate lower yield. A few sit in between, with no advantage on either side.

AGNCN has the same risk profile, and it is not a dramatic outperformer in any single metric. Its best side is that it aligns most optimally between yield, price, and risk across typical market conditions, rather than at a single point in time. AGNCN provides a competitive yield, generally does not require payment way above par, and has fewer structural disadvantages.

data.title: TJX Companies: Premium Will Fade As Retailers Compete More On Price
data.description:
One of the predictable consequences of this year’s market volatility is the fact that investors have made a pronounced shift into lower-risk equities, prioritizing a “flight to safety.” Tech stocks, especially software companies with any potential whiff of AI disruption, are out, while defensive stocks that have more proven ability to keep growing sales amid a tougher consumer environment have been winners.

But the result is that a shift has occurred in which stocks have inflated multiples, and right now countercyclical retail like TJX Companies (TJX), the parent company behind TJ Maxx, Marshall's, and Home Goods. TJX has soared 30% over the past year as investors cheered positive comp sales trends and consistent earnings growth.

We do have to ask ourselves, however, despite recent momentum, can TJX really justify its valuation premiums, especially when so many of its retail peers have seen much more disappointing performance? Investors have crowded into this name due to its perception of safety, but I think elevated multiples certainly remove that margin of safety. I’m initiating TJX at a "Sell" rating.

The Leading Off-Price Chain in the U.S.

Before we dig into valuation and recent performance, let’s get a solid lay of the land on the company’s business. TJX is the corporate umbrella for a portfolio of off-price retail brands.

Its largest brands, TJ Maxx and Marshall’s, which the company collectively refers to as the “Marmaxx” segment in internal reporting, generated 60% of the company’s sales, excluding the TJ Maxx stores that are located outside the United States, which are separately segmented. The company also owns HomeGoods (as the name suggests, a home furnishings and decor retailer) and Sierra, which is an off-price version of REI, as well as a collection of smaller brands.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases TJX’s most recent store footprint as of the end of FY26 (the year for TJX that just ended in January 2026). The company has a whopping 5,214 locations worldwide, of which 3,790 (73%) are in the United States. The company grew its number of retail doors by 2.5% y/y in FY26.

The company is laser-focused on unit expansion. It believes that within its current country base, it has white space to expand to 7,000 overall stores, 34% above its current footprint. In FY27, the company’s capital plans call for opening 146 net new stores, or a slight acceleration to 2.8% location growth this year, with capex in the range of $2.2-$2.3 billion. The company’s major expansion focus for FY27 is Spain, a brand-new country for TJX, with 5 planned stores by the end of the year.

In terms of brand orientation, the TJX Companies focus on everyday value and off-price items. It specifically notes that it wants the shopping experience in a TJX store to be similar to a treasure hunt, in which shoppers might always be surprised about what deals they can discover in-store.

The company targets for its products to be priced between 20-60% below full-price retailers’ regular prices. Price tags on items at TJX stores typically state TJX’s price alongside comparable retail prices so shoppers are aware of their discounts. The company sources products from over 21,000 suppliers across 100 countries and focuses its merchandising and buying on a constantly rotating selection to emphasize newness and discovery as a key reason for loyal shoppers to keep coming back in-store.

The Issues: Huge Valuation, Decelerating Comps, Thin Margins, and a Promotional Environment

Now, we can dig into the crux of my sell rating on TJX. I have to emphasize here that my bearishness on TJX isn’t at all a statement on the company’s execution, which has been superb in a tough consumer landscape.

The snapshot below showcases TJX’s guidance. For the current year, FY27, the company is planning for 2-3% comp sales growth, pretax margins of 11.7%-11.8% that are essentially flat to 11.7% in FY26, and $4.93-$5.02 in pro forma EPS, which reflects an EPS growth range of 4%-6%.

At current share prices near $162, this positions TJX’s valuation at a whopping 32x P/E multiple. This sits substantially above a broad cross-section of retail peers, as shown below:

Here we have to ask a rather elementary yet incredibly pertinent question: Does mid-single-digit EPS growth truly justify a monstrous >30x P/E ratio, let alone such a wide premium versus most peers? In my view, this is a classic showcase of a sector rotation (out of tech and into defensive stocks) that has gone too far.

To me, there are multiple other factors that we need to consider that further dispel how deserving TJX is of a premium. The first is that comp sales are set to slow. Guidance calling for 2%-3% growth represents half the growth rate relative to 5% comps growth in Q4 and FY26 overall.

Of course we cannot expect any company, especially an already established one like TJX, to keep growing at a consistent pace without deceleration in perpetuity. TJX faces tough prior-year comps against strong sales in FY26, which is what makes continuing to grow at ~5% tough. But then this circles me back to my original question: If growth (which is the principal reason TJX was able to amass such a stark premium over peers last year) is set to slow, why shouldn’t the premium evaporate as well?

We also believe there's a growing risk of the retail landscape becoming more promotional. As a reminder, TJX focuses on everyday low prices. It runs sales and deals, but its true differentiation lies in the fact that customers can walk into its store every day, whether it’s Black Friday or any regular day, and still find compelling deals.

The issue is that more and more peers are finding trouble getting customers back in stores in a tough consumer economy, and as such, they’re turning to more frequent promotions to entice customers back in. For example, Kohl’s CEO Michael Bender made the following remarks on the recent Kohl’s Q4 earnings call:

We know consumers are more value conscious and there is opportunity for us to regain share during these windows through strong promotional statements that better align to our customer needs and priorities […]

The company's CFO went on to note on the same call that Kohl's plans to invest some of its gross margin into deeper promotions in calendar 2026:

Gross margin to be flat to down slightly, driven by increased proprietary brand sales offset by increase in digital sales and elevated promotional offers as we drive more value for our customers.

These discounting moves are becoming part of the standard playbook for retail, especially to appeal to value-oriented consumers. It would be a mistake to think that these moves won't impact TJX. The company will have to make a choice between preserving its comp sales trends and market share or investing more of its already-slim gross margins (~31% as of the most recent quarter) to maintain price competitiveness.

Key Takeaways

Again, we emphasize that TJX has executed tremendously well in a tough consumer landscape. The company's focus on everyday low prices has allowed the company to achieve significant comp sales growth in FY26.

That said, a more difficult horizon is ahead in FY27 as the company faces tougher prior-year comps, a more aggressive peer discounting landscape, and the challenge of justifying incredibly high valuation multiples. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: 'AI Security' Emerges As The Next Cybersecurity Theme
data.description:
Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Elvira Kuramshina

Cybersecurity is evolving rapidly as new technologies, threat vectors, and enterprise architectures reshape how organizations defend their systems and data. The WisdomTree Team8 Cybersecurity Index was designed to track how these developments are shaping the future of cybersecurity through key themes. As cybersecurity innovation continues to accelerate, periodically updating these themes helps ensure the index remains aligned with how modern cybersecurity is built, deployed and adopted across the industry.

This is not the first time we have made adjustments; for example, in 2023 we introduced the Layer 8 theme focused on human risk. Today, to reflect the latest developments in the cybersecurity landscape – particularly the maturation of cloud security and the rapid rise of artificial intelligence—we have once again made updates to the index’s key cybersecurity themes (Figure 1a and Figure 1b).

Figure 1a: Exposure of Companies across Cyber Themes in the WisdomTree Team8 Cybersecurity Index

Figure 1b: Description of Cyber Themes in the WisdomTree Team8 Cybersecurity Index

Retiring the Cloud Security Theme

The Cloud Security theme was originally designed to capture companies focused on protecting cloud infrastructure and environments. This included technologies such as Cloud Workload Protection Platforms (CWPP), Cloud Infrastructure Entitlement Management (CIEM), Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM), SaaS Security Posture Management (SSPM), Cloud-Native Application Protection Platforms (CNAPP), Container Security, and Cloud Access Security Brokers (CASB).

However, as cloud adoption has become universal across enterprises, cloud security capabilities have also become widely embedded across the cybersecurity landscape. Today, most cybersecurity vendors offer products that address cloud environments in some capacity. As a result, in our view, cloud security is no longer a meaningful differentiator between cybersecurity vendors, nor does it serve as a strong proxy for identifying companies aligned with emerging cybersecurity growth themes. Reflecting this shift, the Cloud Security theme is being retired as a distinct category within the index.

While the Cloud Security theme is being discontinued, the index introduces AI Security as the next cybersecurity theme, driven by the rapid adoption of AI systems, the expansion of the AI attack surface, and the emergence of specialized AI security solutions.

Introducing AI Security as a Distinct Cybersecurity Theme

Artificial intelligence is rapidly becoming embedded across enterprise software, infrastructure, and workflows. While AI is transforming productivity and enabling new capabilities spanning industries, it is also introducing new security risks and attack surfaces that create unique opportunities for attackers. Several major drivers are accelerating the evolution of the AI security market:

Explosion in the Adoption of AI Systems

Enterprises are rapidly deploying AI tools across software development, knowledge management, customer support, and internal workflows. What began as experimentation with generative AI is increasingly moving into production environments and becoming embedded in core enterprise software systems. Organizations are also beginning to deploy AI agents capable of autonomously interacting with applications, data sources, and external systems, further expanding the operational role of AI within enterprise environments.

New Attack Surfaces in AI Infrastructure

Since AI systems rely on data pipelines, training environments, model repositories and inference infrastructure, each component represents a potential entry point for attackers. Moreover, the growing use of AI agents introduces new security considerations that make them attractive targets. As a result, security teams are increasingly confronting risks that did not exist until recently. These risks include manipulation attacks, where adversaries craft malicious inputs to bypass safeguards or produce unintended outputs; infection attacks, where attackers poison training data or manipulate model behavior to influence system outputs; and exfiltration attacks, which attempt to extract sensitive information such as training data, model parameters, or proprietary algorithms from AI systems.

Rapid Emergence of Specialized AI Security Solutions

Over the past year, a new generation of security companies has emerged, focused specifically on the unique security needs of AI systems and AI-native environments. These solutions are designed to secure AI models and training environments, protect data pipelines and model supply chains, monitor and control AI system behavior, detect vulnerabilities and misuse in AI applications, and enforce governance, compliance, and security controls for enterprise AI deployments.

As these risks and technologies continue to evolve, the cybersecurity market is beginning to recognize AI Security as a distinct category within the broader cybersecurity ecosystem, and for these reasons, the WisdomTree Team8 Cybersecurity Index will now formally recognize AI Security as its own dedicated theme.

Positioning the Index for the Future

The WisdomTree Team8 Cybersecurity Index was designed to track key cybersecurity themes emerging across the industry, and as the industry evolves, so do the key themes shaping its future. By retiring the Cloud Security theme and introducing the AI Security theme, the index continues to reflect key structural developments in the cybersecurity market, helping ensure it remains aligned with the technologies and themes shaping the future of cybersecurity.

Conclusion: How Are Cybersecurity Software Companies Performing in 2026 Relative to the Broader Software Group?

Those investors interested in the strategy defined by the WisdomTree Team8 Cybersecurity Index need to look to the WisdomTree Cybersecurity Fund (WCBR) as the investable vehicle designed to track it. From there, we can look to Figures 2a and 2b to examine what we believe is an important performance-oriented question:

How are cybersecurity software companies, broadly speaking, performing relative to the broader landscape of overall software companies so far in 2026?

Like many others, we are aware of the difficult performance in the software space starting 2026 as the business model is facing challenges from artificial intelligence. Even if we believe that ‘cybersecurity may be different from broader software,’ it’s important to look at what is happening in the market to see if the returns are bearing this out.

In Figures 2a and 2b, the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV), which is designed to track the total return performance before fees and expenses of the S&P North America Expanded Technology Software Index, is used to showcase the performance of ‘broader software.’ IGV has gotten significant attention in 2026 as people have been looking at software specifically, and as of March 26, 2026, it had more than $9.7 billion in assets under management.

In Figure 2a, which is through March 26, 2026, we see that IGV is down roughly 24.5% to start 2026, whereas WCBR is down roughly 9.6%. The story that ‘cybersecurity software may be different’ appears to be playing out. We do understand there is a difference between ‘going down less’ and ‘delivering a positive return,’ but we believe this significant performance difference is telling us something, and we’ll have to monitor to see if this difference continues.

Figure 2a: Cybersecurity Software vs. Broad Software so far in 2026

Figure 2b: Standardized Performance

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically, data are from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of March 18, 2026, but showing returns for the period ended March 17, 2026 for Figures 2a and December 31, 2025 for 2b. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

We believe that what we are seeing in AI so far in 2026 ultimately could create more demands for different cybersecurity solutions—many of which come through software—and we look forward to continuing to see how this picture plays out.

Figure 3: Additional Information

Therefore, we generally do not believe it is possible to make direct fund to fund comparisons in an effort to highlight the benefits of a fund versus another similarly managed fund.

WCBR: There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. The Fund invests in cybersecurity companies, which generate a meaningful part of their revenue from security protocols that prevent intrusion and attacks to systems, networks, applications, computers, and mobile devices. Cybersecurity companies are particularly vulnerable to rapid changes in technology, rapid obsolescence of products and services, the loss of patent, copyright and trademark protections, government regulation and competition, both domestically and internationally. Cybersecurity company stocks, especially those which are internet related, have experienced extreme price and volume fluctuations in the past that have often been unrelated to their operating performance. These companies may also be smaller and less experienced companies, with limited product or service lines, markets or financial resources and fewer experienced management or marketing personnel. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index.
